title: Special Relationship

jokes.text: We are running some reverse 'My Fair Lady' shit here.
jokes.setup: Dan is briefing the team on the strategy for Selina's pub visit — making her look like a regular person.
jokes.punchline: He frames it as 'reverse My Fair Lady shit' — instead of making a commoner into a lady, they're making a lady pretend to be a commoner.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical cultural reference that actually illuminates the political strategy rather than just being a name-drop. The inversion is clever and characterizes Dan as someone who thinks in pop-culture shorthand.

jokes.text: 'Good Mourning, America'... mourning with a 'u.'
jokes.setup: Dan is pitching coverage of Selina at the WWI memorial, dressed in black.
jokes.punchline: He spells out the pun 'mourning with a u' — a groan-worthy but self-aware wordplay on morning/mourning, and then has to explain it, which is itself the joke.
jokes.explanation: The pun is groan-worthy by design; the self-aware explanation ('mourning with a u') adds a layer of meta-humor — Dan knows it's cheesy and says it anyway because he's proud of it. Competent but not special.

jokes.text: I need you to get Selina a meeting with Prince Charles... that 65-year-old fuckin' intern.
jokes.setup: Dan is asking Sue to set up a meeting with Prince Charles.
jokes.punchline: He dismisses the heir to the British throne as a '65-year-old fuckin' intern' — referencing how Charles has been waiting for the throne his entire adult life.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and originality. 'Intern' is the perfect word — it captures Charles's decades of subordinate waiting with a single, perfectly chosen contemporary insult. It's a fresh angle on a well-known situation. One of the episode's best lines. Qualifies as 8+ because it's genuinely surprising and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Dan, if you want to get into the palace at short notice, you're going to have to storm it yourself.
jokes.setup: Dan tells Sue (via phone) to just 'make it happen' re: the palace meeting.
jokes.punchline: Sue flatly tells him to storm it himself — a perfectly dry refusal that reframes his casual command as literally absurd.
jokes.explanation: Very Sue — impeccably dry. The character integration is high because only Sue would respond to an impossible request with this level of unamused literalism.

jokes.text: Tough call. Then again, you're a tough caller.
jokes.setup: Someone says getting Prince Charles's private secretary on the phone will be a tough call.
jokes.punchline: Another person completes the wordplay: 'you're a tough caller' — a meaningless affirmation dressed as a compliment.
jokes.explanation: Weak wordplay — the joke relies on the pun 'tough call/tough caller' which is more awkward than clever. Low character integration; could be anyone saying this.

jokes.text: He had tickets for a West End musical. Something with a duck or a ghost. I don't remember.
jokes.setup: Someone explains why POTUS didn't come to London — he had to close the China deal.
jokes.punchline: A throwaway correction reveals he also had West End tickets — for something with 'a duck or a ghost,' delivered with complete indifference.
jokes.explanation: The vague description of a West End show ('duck or a ghost') is a great piece of throwaway absurdism. The casualness with which it's delivered makes it land harder.

jokes.text: Hey, is FLOTUS having an affair? Not that I would blame her. Have you seen her lately? She's lost a lot of weight. Her neck is like stretched cheese.
jokes.setup: Small talk between Selina and aides about FLOTUS's absence.
jokes.punchline: Selina casually speculates about an affair, pivots to weight loss, then delivers 'her neck is like stretched cheese' — a shockingly specific and revolting image.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+ because 'stretched cheese' is an inspired, grotesque image that lands from nowhere. The escalation from political gossip to petty body-shaming to this surreal simile is perfectly constructed. Pure Selina: catty, specific, and completely oblivious to how awful she sounds. The line also becomes darkly ironic given the FLOTUS reveal at episode's end.

jokes.text: Heroin and Chinese food. / Noodles and needles. / It was actually the solar energy summit in Stockholm.
jokes.setup: Ben and the Deputy PM reminisce about meeting in Hong Kong.
jokes.punchline: Their joking shorthand for Hong Kong ('heroin and Chinese food,' 'noodles and needles') is punctured by the correction that it was actually a solar energy summit in Stockholm.
jokes.explanation: A very tight three-beat exchange. The 'noodles and needles' as a callback/echo of 'heroin and Chinese food' is perfectly constructed, and the mundane correction ('solar energy summit in Stockholm') is a great deflation. Structure is the strength here.

jokes.text: That's the one, that's the one. Same vibe.
jokes.setup: They've just been corrected that it was a solar energy summit in Stockholm, not Hong Kong.
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately agrees — 'same vibe' — refusing to acknowledge the enormous difference between a heroin/food capital and a Scandinavian climate conference.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on joke 8. 'Same vibe' is an extraordinarily economical punchline — two words that reveal everything about Ben's character (unflappable, slightly drunk on his own cynicism) and extend the absurdity maximally.

jokes.text: 100 years since the Great War is so my area, Amy. Death, glory, folly, tragedy. It's got the four main mood groups.
jokes.setup: Mike is pitching his WWI speech to Amy, describing it as his 'masterpiece.'
jokes.punchline: 'It's got the four main mood groups' — treating literary pathos like nutritional food groups. The confidence with which he delivers nonsense.
jokes.explanation: Mike's self-satisfaction is perfectly rendered. 'Four main mood groups' is a great comic non-sequitur — the specificity of 'four' and the pseudo-scientific framing of emotions make it land. Pure Mike: earnest, slightly dim, convinced of his own profundity.

jokes.text: She can lie to the Brits and go straight to confession.
jokes.setup: The team discusses the secret TSO meeting with the German chancellor at a cathedral — the Brits don't know about it.
jokes.punchline: Amy notes that meeting in a cathedral is conveniently located for immediate confession after lying to allies.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical observation that nails the moral bankruptcy of the political world the show inhabits. The cathedral setting being used for deception and the proximity to absolution is a nice dark irony.

jokes.text: In related news, Ray's talking. Oh, God. To smart people? He's currently with a woman from the Bank of England. It's like watching a goat trying to use an ATM.
jokes.setup: Amy mentions Ray is talking to someone. Ben asks the perfect follow-up: 'To smart people?'
jokes.punchline: Amy confirms it's a Bank of England economist and delivers 'goat trying to use an ATM' — a vivid and specific image of hopeless incompetence.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+ on multiple counts. Ben's 'To smart people?' is a perfect economy joke — one question that says everything about Ray's reputation. Then 'goat trying to use an ATM' is a genuinely inspired simile: specific, visual, absurd but comprehensible. The two-punch structure (Ben's fear confirmed, then the image) is excellent comedy construction.

jokes.text: Ray's economics monologue: 'Money is just a concept... it's not like muscles in your arms.' / 'I think that's quite a superficial analysis.' / 'You know what intrinsic means?' / 'I try to learn a word a day. I love words very ardently.'
jokes.setup: We cut to Ray actually speaking to the Bank of England economist, as described.
jokes.punchline: Ray's 'economics' is anti-intellectual gibberish; his proud declaration that he 'tries to learn a word a day' and loves words 'very ardently' confirms he has just learned 'intrinsic' today.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to joke 12's setup. The escalating absurdity of Ray's 'philosophy' and the reveal of 'I love words very ardently' (clearly demonstrating he just learned 'ardently') is terrific character comedy. The Bank of England woman's pained politeness amplifies the cringe.

jokes.text: I hope that 'honestly' isn't one of those words that's lost its meaning whilst traveling the Atlantic.
jokes.setup: Selina says 'honestly, we haven't made a decision' about the TSO location — while clearly lying.
jokes.punchline: The Deputy PM skewers her with surgical politeness — noting that 'honestly' may have changed meaning crossing the Atlantic, i.e., she's lying.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The British diplomatic passive-aggressive register is perfect — it's an accusation of lying dressed in impeccable politeness. 'Whilst traveling the Atlantic' makes it specifically about Anglo-American relations. It's devastatingly economical and entirely earned. The irony is multi-layered: language, diplomacy, and hypocrisy all skewered at once. Clearly a 8.5+ moment.

jokes.text: The U.S. doesn't spy on its allies. / We collect data. / Same thing. / Oh, no.
jokes.setup: Selina reassures the Deputy PM the US doesn't spy on allies.
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately corrects: 'We collect data.' The Deputy PM says 'same thing.' Selina's 'Oh, no' is a desperate, unconvincing denial.
jokes.explanation: A four-beat exchange that's structurally perfect. The timing of Ben's correction undermining Selina, the Deputy PM's flat 'same thing,' and Selina's impotent 'oh, no' are all timed to perfection on the page. Topically resonant (post-Snowden era). Near 8+ territory for structure.

jokes.text: Gary, what do you think? [Gary clearly panics] Gary likes to keep his cards close to his chest. [Gary] No, please, do share. I'm just 'collecting data.'
jokes.setup: The Deputy PM draws Gary into the spying debate, presumably knowing Gary has nothing to add.
jokes.punchline: Gary's visible panic is bailed out by Selina who covers for him, but the Deputy PM lands another cutting 'I'm just collecting data' callback — using Selina's own team's language against her.
jokes.explanation: A good tripling of the spying/data joke. Gary's panic (inferred from the transcript structure) is a great reaction beat, and the Deputy PM's callback closes the sequence elegantly.

jokes.text: I think it's very nuanced. And I think there's a lot of different sides to a lot of different topics that are out there that I think we could spend some time... Gary, I need that lipstick.
jokes.setup: Gary has been invited to share his view on the spying debate.
jokes.punchline: Gary launches into a meaningless word salad of filler phrases, building to nothing — then Selina cuts him off mid-sentence to ask for lipstick, reducing him to a handbag.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy. Gary's word salad perfectly captures his terrified emptiness when put on the spot, and Selina cutting him off mid-sentence to ask for lipstick is the perfect humiliation — it confirms he is purely a body-man/accessory, not a political aide. High character integration score because this works ONLY because it's Gary.

jokes.text: Madam out there hobnobbing, us here in the servants' quarters. It's like 'Downton Abbey.' 'McClintock, Lady Selina wishes to eat a Cornish pasty in the paddock this evening. Post-haste, you bugger!' / I love that voice. It makes me feel inadequate and horny. The dream.
jokes.setup: Mike and Gary are waiting in what feels like a back room while Selina mingles.
jokes.punchline: Mike does a posh English butler impression; Gary's response — 'it makes me feel inadequate and horny' — is startlingly honest, immediately redeemed by 'the dream' as if that's the ideal emotional state.
jokes.explanation: Gary's line is the standout: the non-sequitur of 'inadequate and horny' followed by the breezy 'the dream' is a wonderful character beat. It's slightly absurd oversharing but completely in keeping with Gary's weird devotion. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Ray listing his book titles: '60-Day Shred,' 'Get A Bod Like God,' 'My Name Is Ray, You're Okay,' 'The Greater Glory In 20 Days'
jokes.setup: Peter Mitchell politely asks if he'd know any of Ray's titles.
jokes.punchline: Ray lists increasingly absurd self-help book titles that reveal his empty pomposity — especially 'My Name Is Ray, You're Okay' (a riff on 'I'm OK, You're OK') and 'Get A Bod Like God.'
jokes.explanation: 'Get A Bod Like God' and 'My Name Is Ray, You're Okay' are genuinely funny title constructions — the latter being a perfect parody of the self-help genre. The escalating absurdity of the list and Peter Mitchell's silent reaction sell this.

jokes.text: Wasn't it Oscar Wilde who said, 'Dan's a fucking terrible campaign manager'?
jokes.setup: Someone is criticizing Dan's work as campaign manager.
jokes.punchline: The Wildean attribution is clearly false and the joke is the absurdity of attributing a modern political insult to Oscar Wilde — while also being a pointed dig at Dan.
jokes.explanation: A nicely constructed joke — the Wildean attribution is both a London setting joke (Wilde being Anglo-Irish) and an efficient way to skewer Dan. The formula of false attribution + contemporary insult is well-executed.

jokes.text: I would like to shoot him, but there are no guns in this country.
jokes.setup: Someone expresses frustration with Dan.
jokes.punchline: The deadpan observation that gun violence fantasies are thwarted specifically by British gun laws.
jokes.explanation: A competent dark joke — the American/British cultural contrast is pointed but the concept isn't especially fresh. Works better in context (London setting).

jokes.text: Steven, this is Jonah Ryan reporting to Team Maddox. I'm in London Town, and it's going to be a 'right royal cockney barrel of turnips' when I dish the dirt on Selina Meyer. Yes, sir, I'll stop using the accent.
jokes.setup: Jonah is on the phone with his Maddox handler, speaking in a terrible fake British accent.
jokes.punchline: 'A right royal cockney barrel of turnips' — a completely mangled attempt at British slang that means nothing — followed by him being immediately told to stop.
jokes.explanation: 'Right royal cockney barrel of turnips' is a great piece of Jonah-specific gibberish — it sounds British but is actually meaningless, perfectly capturing his confident incompetence. Being told to stop immediately is a clean punchline.

jokes.text: Hello, Jonah. / Hello, sir. / Just buying some tickets for Madame Tussauds. / You're an imbecile.
jokes.setup: Peter Mitchell spots Jonah loitering.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's cover story for lurking outside is that he's buying Madame Tussauds tickets — a pathetically transparent tourist excuse. Peter Mitchell's flat 'You're an imbecile' is the perfect response.
jokes.explanation: The Madame Tussauds detail is brilliantly specific — only Jonah would choose this as a cover story. And 'You're an imbecile' as an unvarnished assessment with no diplomatic softening from a Deputy PM is wonderful.

jokes.text: I can imagine Harry Potter getting loaded in here.
jokes.setup: Selina is touring a charming British pub, performing folksy enthusiasm.
jokes.punchline: Her idea of relatable British small talk is imagining a fictional child getting drunk in the pub.
jokes.explanation: A good Selina joke — her attempt to be charming is immediately awkward because she's imagining a child drinking. Sets up the subsequent exchange perfectly.

jokes.text: The extended Harry Potter argument: fictional child vs. actor, wizard not witch, the bartender carefully correcting Selina at each step while she gets progressively more wrong.
jokes.setup: The barman gently notes Harry Potter is underage. Selina pivots to 'the actor is a grown man.' The barman notes that's the actor, not the character. Selina calls Harry Potter a 'child witch.' The barman corrects: wizard.
jokes.punchline: Selina's attempt to recover each correction makes things worse — 'fictional story of a child witch' — until she's corrected on the most basic fact about one of the world's most famous fictional characters.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+ as a sustained cringe escalation sequence. Each beat makes Selina look worse: she can't distinguish fiction from reality, gets the basic wizard/witch distinction wrong, and her attempt to sound culturally aware reveals the opposite. The barman's patient corrections amplify the humiliation. The structure builds beautifully.

jokes.text: 'Jonah Bond, double-o-fuck off.'
jokes.setup: Amy learns Jonah is in London, apparently to spy on Selina for the Maddox team.
jokes.punchline: 'Jonah Bond, double-o-fuck off' — a Bond reference where his license number is an instruction to get lost.
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical — five words that do double duty as a Bond parody and an insult. The 'double-o' construction is perfectly adapted. Highly quotable. A very clean, well-crafted line.

jokes.text: The West Ham / 'What kind of ham?' exchange. Selina repeatedly misunderstands 'West Ham United' and tries to reframe it as American football.
jokes.setup: The barman mentions he's a West Ham fan. Selina hears 'ham' and is confused.
jokes.punchline: Selina insists on explaining that Americans 'have football there,' suggesting British football needs a different name — while the barman patiently maintains: 'Football.'
jokes.explanation: The soccer/football debate is well-worn territory, and 'what kind of ham' is a predictable mishearing joke. What saves it is the escalation — Selina doesn't just make the mistake once, she doubles down. Competent but not special.

jokes.text: Barman suggests a pinot noir, then says 'I'm joking. He won't.' Then: 'I think you look like a bitter person, maybe.' [pause] 'Which is a type of beer. Don't worry. It's just a joke. Just playing. Bitter is a horrible beer.'
jokes.setup: The barman offers Selina a drink recommendation.
jokes.punchline: He suggests pinot noir as a joke, then calls her a 'bitter person,' has to explain 'bitter' is a beer, and then undermines the whole thing by adding 'bitter is a horrible beer.'
jokes.explanation: The barman's self-undermining — explaining his own joke and then calling bitter horrible — creates a believable awkward social moment. Slight overstuff (one beat too many) but works as cringe.

jokes.text: Well, she's gonna drink it and she's gonna smile, Gary. / I've got some peppermints. / I've got digestive enzymes.
jokes.setup: The team discusses Selina having to drink bitter beer she hates.
jokes.punchline: Gary and Mike compete to offer the most pathetic pre-emptive remedies — peppermints and digestive enzymes — as if the real problem is Selina's digestion and not her political image.
jokes.explanation: A good character beat — Gary and Mike both trying to be helpful in the most ineffectual, overprepared way possible. The parallel structure (peppermints/digestive enzymes) is neat.

jokes.text: The vice president is now going to 'pull a pint.' / Come on down. / I just said that. / Yep, yep.
jokes.setup: Dan announces Selina's pint-pulling to the room.
jokes.punchline: Selina has just said the same thing herself, and Dan's 'I just said that' is met with 'yep, yep' — a small humiliation of Selina being talked over in her own event.
jokes.explanation: A small but authentic moment of Selina being steamrolled by her own staff. Works as character comedy but is not a standout.

jokes.text: The pub crowd chanting 'Down in one' / Selina mishearing it as 'Daniwah' and chanting back enthusiastically.
jokes.setup: Selina pulls a pint and the crowd chants 'down in one' (drink it in one go). Selina mishears it as a Japanese-sounding chant.
jokes.punchline: Selina chants 'Daniwah!' back at the crowd, who laugh and chant it back — she thinks she's landed a cultural moment but she's completely lost.
jokes.explanation: The episode's setpiece comic disaster. The concept is simple but the execution is perfect — Selina confidently doubling down on a mistake in public, creating an embarrassing 'catchphrase' she doesn't understand. The crowd laughing at her while she thinks they're cheering with her is peak Veep cringe. High impact because the visual of Selina chanting 'Daniwah!' is supremely funny and memorable.

jokes.text: They're eating this up. I think we just landed a catchphrase. / They're not saying 'Daniwah.' They're saying 'down in one.' / Moron.
jokes.setup: Dan thinks 'Daniwah' is a genuine viral moment for Selina.
jokes.punchline: Amy explains they're saying 'down in one.' Then 'Moron.' Perfect economy.
jokes.explanation: Amy's 'Moron.' is a perfect one-word punchline — all the weight of her contempt compressed into a single word. The character dynamic (Amy having to explain to Dan something he should have caught) is well-exploited.

jokes.text: They're laughing at her like a toddler they taught to swear.
jokes.setup: The pub crowd is chanting 'Daniwah' back at Selina.
jokes.punchline: Amy's simile — 'toddler they taught to swear' — perfectly captures the patronizing glee of the crowd watching Selina's oblivious mistake.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Toddler they taught to swear' is a genuinely original and perfectly precise simile — it captures both the crowd's amused condescension and Selina's unknowing participation in her own humiliation. Highly quotable and original. Comfortably 8+.

jokes.text: 'Daniwah' is trending. It's number 2 under Kate Middleton's bony ass.
jokes.setup: Dan checks social media after the pub visit.
jokes.punchline: The trending position is revealed — it's beaten only by Kate Middleton's anatomy, which is both a celebrity-gossip satire and a specific, slightly transgressive image.
jokes.explanation: A solid button on the Daniwah sequence. The specificity of Kate Middleton's trending anatomy is good — it's irreverent and topical. Not exceptional but very competent.

jokes.text: Siri, how many horses died in the First World War? / Why are you asking that? / For the speech. / No. I'm not talking about horses. Cancel search, Siri.
jokes.setup: Selina is supposedly prepping for the WWI memorial speech.
jokes.punchline: She's asking Siri about horse casualties — a completely off-message research direction that Mike immediately shuts down.
jokes.explanation: A nice character beat — Selina's historical research process being Siri queries about horses is perfectly in keeping with her approach to gravitas (surface-level, easily distracted). Mike's 'Cancel search, Siri' is a good button.

jokes.text: Siri, how many horses died in the First World War?
jokes.setup: Selina is preparing her WWI memorial speech.
jokes.punchline: The specificity of asking Siri about horse casualties — and Mike's horrified reaction — reveals Selina's speech preparation method as googling random facts on her phone.
jokes.explanation: The image of the Vice President asking Siri about horse casualties for a solemn memorial speech is a perfect satirical compression of how modern political speeches get made. Mike's 'Cancel search, Siri' is the ideal button.

jokes.text: You're gonna look so stunning at the war service, ma'am. Those guys are gonna wish they weren't dead.
jokes.setup: Gary is complimenting Selina on her appearance before the WWI memorial.
jokes.punchline: 'Those guys are gonna wish they weren't dead' — a gallows-humor compliment so tone-deaf it loops back to being funny, implying the fallen soldiers would prefer to be alive just to see Selina.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines — and pure Gary. His absolute obliviousness to the dark implications of the compliment, delivered in total sincerity at a WWI memorial, is perfect character comedy. 'Those guys are gonna wish they weren't dead' is genuinely shocking and funny. A Gary line that only Gary could deliver. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Hat selection: 'Do you think the bigger one? Don't you think the other hat is a little too hatty?' / 'Britain is the kingdom of the hats.' / 'I have never heard that phrase.' / 'It's a well-known phrase.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Ray debate which hat to wear to the memorial.
jokes.punchline: Ray authoritatively declares 'Britain is the kingdom of the hats' — a phrase he has clearly just invented — and then insists it's 'a well-known phrase' when challenged.
jokes.explanation: Ray confidently inventing and defending a fake common phrase ('kingdom of the hats') is a great character beat. Also Selina's 'too hatty' is a nice throwaway — she's invented her own vocabulary. Good character work all around.

jokes.text: The 'or nothing at all' / hat scene implied sexual encounter between Selina and Ray — played entirely through innuendo and Selina closing the door on Gary.
jokes.setup: Ray suggests wearing the hat 'with something else' or 'nothing at all.' Gary is in the room.
jokes.punchline: The innuendo is thick, Gary is visibly confused/uncomfortable, and Selina closes the door on him — confirming without stating that something is about to happen.
jokes.explanation: The implication is handled with appropriate lightness — more cringe than explicit. Gary's exclusion (and the door closing on him) is the visual punchline, reinforcing his status as perpetually on the outside of Selina's actual life.

jokes.text: Dan: 'I am Dan With A Plan Egan, so if I can't work, then you can't work...' / Amy cuts him off: 'Call him back. We've got to go in there and talk to Selina. / That's... I was doing a little role-playing.'
jokes.setup: Dan tries to deliver an inspirational speech to the team about his role.
jokes.punchline: Amy interrupts mid-pomposity; Dan's defense — 'I was doing a little role-playing' — is baffling and implies he was pretending to be himself.
jokes.explanation: Dan's 'I was doing a little role-playing' is a nice bit of baffling non-defense — he was playing himself, apparently. Good character work showing his self-mythology.

jokes.text: Like the Charles Manson defense?
jokes.setup: Dan claims he was 'role-playing.'
jokes.punchline: Amy compares this to the Charles Manson defense — darkly suggesting role-playing as a legal/PR defense strategy is associated with mass murderers.
jokes.explanation: A very economical dark joke — the comparison is unexpected and lands from nowhere. Amy's casual willingness to invoke Manson in a trivial office dispute is very much her character.

jokes.text: Nicely done, 'Obi-Wah Kenobi.'
jokes.setup: Jonah has somehow got himself into a newspaper photo.
jokes.punchline: 'Obi-Wah Kenobi' — a Star Wars pun built on the 'Daniwah' running gag.
jokes.explanation: A clever callback that earns its place — 'Obi-Wah Kenobi' works both as a callback to 'Daniwah' and as a Star Wars reference appropriate to Jonah's looming, eccentric presence. The pun construction is tight. One of the better wordplay moments in the episode.

jokes.text: I can handle one paper, guys. And you know this about me. I can chop this thing in two like a disputed kitten.
jokes.setup: Dan is dismissing one newspaper story as manageable.
jokes.punchline: 'I can chop this thing in two like a disputed kitten' — a completely mangled reference to the biblical story of Solomon (who threatened to chop a disputed baby), with 'kitten' substituting for 'baby' in a bizarre, deflating way.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+ because the mangled Solomon reference is both specific and original. 'Disputed kitten' is a genius substitution — it deflates the biblical gravitas, makes Dan sound like he half-remembers the story, and is absurdly specific. This is Dan's character perfectly: over-confident, slightly wrong, memorable. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Very proud of it, ma'am. I gave this one 100% effort. As I do everything I write for you.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about the WWI speech. Mike preemptively defends it.
jokes.punchline: The protestation that he gives everything 100% — which implies he usually doesn't — is exactly the kind of thing someone says when they know they're under threat.
jokes.explanation: A mild character joke — Mike's insecurity leaking through his defensiveness. Not standout but true to the character.

jokes.text: Speech critique exchange: 'Too effy for me' / 'That sentence is impossible to comprehend' / 'I could normalfy this for you' / 'No. No, no, no.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Ray critique Mike's carefully crafted WWI speech.
jokes.punchline: Ray offers to 'normalfy' the speech — a made-up word for making things normal, from the man who just demonstrated he can't speak — and Mike's escalating horror at each suggestion.
jokes.explanation: 'Normalfy' is a great Ray-specific malapropism. The scene works as a sustained cringe because Mike's masterpiece is being evaluated by people who don't understand it, by design.

jokes.text: Why does he have access to the 'G' folder? I don't even have access to the 'G' folder. / Yes, you do, Mike. We all do. It's the general file on the iCloud. / Everything was much easier when it was just floppy discs.
jokes.setup: Ray has printed Mike's speech from the 'G' folder.
jokes.punchline: Mike's indignant question reveals he doesn't know about a file system everyone else knows about; Amy deflates him; Mike's nostalgic retreat to 'floppy discs' confirms his technological displacement.
jokes.explanation: 'Everything was much easier when it was just floppy discs' is a great character button — Mike's technological anxiety expressed as genuine nostalgia. The 'G folder' confusion is good setup for it.

jokes.text: To paraphrase 'My Fair Lady,' we need to get to the fuckin' church on time.
jokes.setup: The team needs to stop debating the speech and get moving.
jokes.punchline: Dan invokes 'My Fair Lady' — which he referenced at the episode's start as 'reverse My Fair Lady shit' — as an authoritative source, mangling the actual song title ('Get Me to the Church on Time') into an imperative.
jokes.explanation: A decent callback to the opening My Fair Lady reference. The misquotation (the actual line is 'Get Me to the Church on Time') is either intentional or characteristically Dan — probably both. Works as a callback but not a standout.

jokes.text: Everything was much easier when it was just floppy discs.
jokes.setup: The team debates iCloud and file access.
jokes.punchline: Mike's wistful nostalgia for floppy discs — as if they solved anything — is perfectly misguided and out of touch.
jokes.explanation: Quick character beat — Mike's technophobia wrapped in false nostalgia. The floppy disc detail is perfectly chosen for maximum datedness.

jokes.text: Ray's alternative speech: 'There's a whole lotta guys who never came home. Good guys. Here's to those guys.' / Holy fuckin' Christ. What is that, Ray?
jokes.setup: Ray has been asked to offer his 'working-class' touch on the WWI memorial speech.
jokes.punchline: His rewrite for 16 million WWI dead: three sentences of beer-toast banality.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+ — Ray's speech rewrite is a masterclass in character-specific comedy. The contrast between Mike's careful pathos ('beneath the dark soil of Passchendaele...') and Ray's beer-toast simplicity ('Here's to those guys') is perfectly calibrated. The rhythm of the three sentences — short, declarative, meaningless — is itself funny. Selina's 'Holy fuckin' Christ' is the perfect reaction.

jokes.text: Despite being told he is fired, Ray says 'I get you' and then 'I'm so pleased we had this talk. Let's see where we all are in a month or so.'
jokes.setup: Kent is firing Ray in extremely plain terms.
jokes.punchline: Ray interprets being fired as a productive dialogue and schedules a check-in — demonstrating he either doesn't understand or refuses to accept reality.
jokes.explanation: Ray's confidence-coaching speak weaponized against the reality of his firing is great character comedy. 'Let's see where we all are in a month or so' from someone just fired is beautifully oblivious.

jokes.text: I'll hold off that long streak of deputy piss while she's in there.
jokes.setup: Selina is about to meet the German chancellor secretly; Ben offers to run interference with the Deputy PM.
jokes.punchline: 'Long streak of deputy piss' as a description of the Deputy PM — a deeply specific and visceral British insult delivered with casual authority.
jokes.explanation: 'Long streak of deputy piss' is quintessential Veep — specific, visceral, anatomical, and delivered without flourish. Ben's casual contempt for everyone around him is perfectly expressed. High quotability.

jokes.text: Selina delivers Ray's version of the speech at the actual memorial: 'Let's remember the guys who never came home. Here's to the good guys. Boy, they were good guys, weren't they? They were.'
jokes.setup: Selina said she would stick with Mike's words at the memorial. She is now at the memorial.
jokes.punchline: She delivers Ray's beer-toast speech — which she explicitly rejected — at the actual memorial, making it exponentially worse in context.
jokes.explanation: The best structural joke in the episode. The callback to Ray's speech is perfectly set up by Selina rejecting it; her delivering it anyway (with additions like 'Boy, they were good guys, weren't they? They were.') at the actual memorial is a devastating comedic payoff. The extra improvised line — a rhetorical question about dead WWI soldiers — amplifies the cringe perfectly. A textbook example of Veep's earned escalation. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Jonah waiting in the car park, nervously clarifying to a stranger: 'No, I'm not waiting on a guy to have sex with. I am waiting on a guy to talk... he's a reporter.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is loitering in a car park to meet a reporter. A stranger asks if he's lost his car.
jokes.punchline: Jonah unpromptedly volunteers that he's not there for gay sex — a disclaimer no one asked for, which raises more questions than it answers.
jokes.explanation: A classic Jonah over-explanation — his attempt to seem normal makes everything worse. The stranger's continued engagement ('I get it, sir') is a good secondary beat.

jokes.text: No. I... I'm not waiting on a guy to have sex with. I am waiting on a guy to talk... he's a reporter.
jokes.setup: A man asks Jonah (loitering in a car park) if he's 'waiting on a guy.'
jokes.punchline: Jonah immediately assumes a gay implication and over-explains to dispel it — the clarification itself digging the hole deeper.
jokes.explanation: Serviceable Jonah cringe — his homophobic over-explanation is in character but the joke relies on a somewhat worn premise. Gets a solid laugh but doesn't push beyond the expected.

jokes.text: Why do you want to meet in a fucking car park? / I don't know, I just thought 'Watergate, Deep Throat.'
jokes.setup: The reporter calls Jonah out for the cloak-and-dagger car park meeting.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's justification is 'Watergate, Deep Throat' — he has modeled his gossip-leaking on a constitutional crisis, which is both grandiose and completely wrong-headed.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's self-image as a Deep Throat figure leaking trainer gossip is perfectly calibrated pomposity. The reference is well-placed given the London/American spy subplot.

jokes.text: Eight pounds for two coffees? Thank you, America.
jokes.setup: They've moved to a coffee shop.
jokes.punchline: The reporter sarcastically blames America for London's expensive coffee prices.
jokes.explanation: A weak observational joke — the expensive London coffee bit is well-worn and the 'Thank you, America' attribution is forced. Lowest tier in this episode.

jokes.text: She sounds like Mary Poppins on all fours. / You do realize Mary Poppins would be over 100 by now? / Yeah, and I'd love to sweep her chimney, eh?
jokes.setup: Jonah describes his hot British source as sounding like Mary Poppins.
jokes.punchline: Rob's correction (Mary Poppins would be over 100) is met by Jonah doubling down with a chimney-sweeping sexual metaphor for an elderly fictional character.
jokes.explanation: The age calculation correction is a smart move, and Jonah's refusal to abandon his fantasy even given that information is very Jonah. 'Sweep her chimney' landing on a centenarian fictional nanny is a weird, committed joke.

jokes.text: You do realize Mary Poppins would be over 100 by now? Yeah, and I'd love to sweep her chimney, eh? If you know what I mean?
jokes.setup: The reporter points out Mary Poppins' age as an objection to Jonah's fantasy.
jokes.punchline: Jonah doubles down — 'I'd love to sweep her chimney' — refusing to let the century-old fictional nanny detail deflate his enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: The escalation works — the reporter tries to logically shut down the fantasy, Jonah just escalates. 'Sweep her chimney' is a perfectly chosen euphemism given the character. Solid callback escalation.

jokes.text: So you Googled it. / No, I didn't just Google it. I found this thing called the 'Internet Archive'... / So you Googled it. / I Googled 'Internet Archive,' yes.
jokes.setup: Jonah claims to have done real investigative research into Ray's deleted blog.
jokes.punchline: He Googled 'Internet Archive' — which is itself just Googling something. The repetition of 'so you Googled it' and Jonah's convoluted concession is a neat two-beat structure.
jokes.explanation: A tight, well-constructed exchange. The repetition of 'so you Googled it' is structurally sound, and 'I Googled Internet Archive, yes' is a perfect concession — Jonah fully understanding the point being made while being unable to escape it.

jokes.text: Oh, you overheard. You speak German? / Yes... ja, as they say in Germany.
jokes.setup: Ben challenges the Deputy PM on how he overheard the German chancellor's conversation.
jokes.punchline: 'Ja, as they say in Germany' — an absurdly unnecessary clarification that 'ja' is German — is perfectly condescending diplomatic deflection.
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully British condescension — 'ja, as they say in Germany' is simultaneously obvious information delivered as if educating someone. The characterization of the Deputy PM as politely insufferable is consistent and perfectly executed.

jokes.text: German language exchange: Ben and the Deputy PM confess their German is rusty. The DPM says 'Ich muss wieder zur schule gehen.' Ben nods and says 'absolutely.' Then: 'Good times.' / 'At school?' / 'What? I said I should go back to school.'
jokes.setup: Both men are covering for Selina's secret cathedral meeting. The Deputy PM speaks German to demonstrate he overheard; Ben nods along.
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'absolutely' and 'good times' response to 'I should go back to school' reveals he either understood nothing or was bluffing along — both readings are funny.
jokes.explanation: A nice piece of sustained awkwardness — two diplomats bluffing through a German language moment. Ben's 'good times' response to a sentence about school is the highlight.

jokes.text: Is the vice president in the Lady Chapel with the German chancellor? / No, I told you before, she's on the phone. / Is that not quite clearly her hat? / That's not her hat. / It's definitely her hat. I'll tell you how I know — I remember thinking 'What a hideous hat.' It is like she stuck her head in a swan and it's exploded.
jokes.setup: Ben insists Selina is on the phone. Her hat is visible in the Lady Chapel.
jokes.punchline: The DPM identifies the hat by remembering thinking 'What a hideous hat' and describes it as Selina having stuck her head in a swan that exploded.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. 'She stuck her head in a swan and it's exploded' is a strikingly original, specifically British (swans being royal property) image that is both insulting and vivid. The delivery — as calm diplomatic evidence-gathering — is perfect. The callback to the hat debate earlier gives it extra payoff. Easily 8.5+.

jokes.text: It's like riding ein bike.
jokes.setup: The Deputy PM is making a point about German language retention.
jokes.punchline: He switches to 'ein' (German 'a') mid-idiom — mixing languages in the very sentence about speaking German, undercutting his own point.
jokes.explanation: Delightful structural irony — while claiming German stays with you, he mixes in 'ein' which proves he's only half-competent. Economy is as high as it gets (three words). Earns 8.

jokes.text: Well, du lugst. / Hilarious. / It means 'you're lying.' / I know.
jokes.setup: The DPM and Ben have been going back and forth about the hat and the meeting.
jokes.punchline: The Deputy PM says 'du lugst' (you're lying) and immediately translates it as if Ben wouldn't know, which is itself condescending. Ben's 'I know' confirms the condescension was unwarranted.
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical — the Deputy PM switching to German to say 'you're lying' and then translating it as if Ben is stupid, while Ben simply says 'I know,' is a perfect four-word deflation. The British diplomacy-as-weaponized-politeness theme lands again.

jokes.text: Okay, so we're all perfectly comfortable standing here pretending that we can't quite clearly see the vice president in the Lady Chapel with the German chancellor finalizing a deal about the TSO? / I wouldn't say 'comfortable.'
jokes.setup: The fiction that Selina isn't in the Lady Chapel has been exhausted.
jokes.punchline: The DPM lays out the entire absurd situation in one sentence; Ben's response is to quibble only with 'comfortable' — refusing to deny the rest.
jokes.explanation: Ben's 'I wouldn't say comfortable' is a perfect diplomatic concession — by quibbling only with the emotional word, he implicitly confirms everything else. A masterpiece of saying everything by denying almost nothing. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Okay, so unelected loony has the ear and minge of the vice president. He says that fat people — fat kids — fat kiddies have 'sinned' in a past life.
jokes.setup: Rob the reporter is processing Ray's blog post about obesity.
jokes.punchline: His escalating self-correction ('fat people — fat kids — fat kiddies') reveals he's workshopping which framing will be most scandalous, while 'ear and minge' is a startlingly frank description of Selina's relationship with Ray.
jokes.explanation: 'Ear and minge' is a brilliantly crude compression of political and sexual access. The self-correcting escalation from 'fat people' to 'fat kiddies' perfectly captures how a tabloid journalist thinks about maximizing outrage.

jokes.text: You know how upsetting that's gonna be to fat people? All of America is fat people. That's all we have. All of our children weigh 300 fucking pounds.
jokes.setup: Rob the reporter is analyzing how damaging Ray's obesity post will be.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's political analysis of the American fat electorate is both statistically absurd ('all of our children weigh 300 pounds') and delivered with the confidence of a genuine strategist.
jokes.explanation: Jonah accidentally being right about American electoral demographics while being absurdly wrong about the specifics ('300 pounds') is good character comedy. The 'all we have' framing is nihilistic and funny.

jokes.text: We've got fat kids too. I've got a fat kid. Well, I can see her... I mean, that's, uh... right.
jokes.setup: Rob is relating to the fat kids story by mentioning his own fat kid.
jokes.punchline: He starts to physically describe his fat child ('well, I can see her...') and then catches himself — the implication being she's large enough to be visible from across the room.
jokes.explanation: The self-interruption is well-timed — the implication of 'I can see her' as a gauge of size is dark and funny. Good cringe economy.

jokes.text: We'll ambush the vice president. We'll get photos of her being surrounded by fat kids dressed as Satan.
jokes.setup: Rob is planning how to exploit the Ray Whelans story visually.
jokes.punchline: His plan — fat children dressed as devils surrounding Selina — is both a practical tabloid stunt and an absurdist image of extraordinary specificity.
jokes.explanation: A great absurdist image that's also believable as tabloid journalism. 'Fat kids dressed as Satan' is specific enough to be genuinely funny — the image of Selina surrounded by devil-costumed overweight children at a diplomatic event is vivid and memorable.

jokes.text: Press conference ambush: 'Or would you like to comment?' / 'Well, I don't know what story it is you're referring to...' / 'He wrote an essay saying obese children are possessed by the devil as a punishment for past sins.'
jokes.setup: A journalist asks about Ray Whelans at the press conference.
jokes.punchline: Selina's diplomatic ignorance is immediately demolished when the actual content of the essay is read aloud — she cannot maintain plausible deniability against 'obese children are possessed by the devil.'
jokes.explanation: The trap closing on Selina in public is well-executed cringe. The specific wording of the essay (possessed by the devil) makes it funnier than a generic controversy would be.

jokes.text: Way to go, Dan. / Way to go, Ray. / Yeah, just throw the blame around. Not at me, though. It's not my fault.
jokes.setup: The press conference is going badly thanks to the Ray story.
jokes.punchline: Blame is thrown, Dan immediately exempts himself — the political instinct to avoid blame kicking in before the crisis has even been assessed.
jokes.explanation: A competent character moment — Dan's reflex self-exoneration is true to character but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I was wondering if you were available this evening... I've found a very charming gastropub. Uh, that sounds lovely. Brilliant. Text the details to my cell... mobile.
jokes.setup: Jonah's British reporter contact seems to be a woman he has a crush on.
jokes.punchline: Jonah asks her to dinner, she accepts warmly, and he almost says 'cell' before self-correcting to 'mobile' — the tiny Americanism correction being an attempt to impress her.
jokes.explanation: The 'cell/mobile' correction is a nice small character touch — Jonah trying to impress by going native. Not explosive but characterful.

jokes.text: It's called 'cosmic balance.' Not anything the Buddha didn't talk about. / Yeah, well, Buddha's got a big fat ass, Ray.
jokes.setup: Ray tries to defend his obesity theology by invoking Buddhism.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to the Buddha defense is to attack the Buddha's physique.
jokes.explanation: A strikingly good line — Selina's willingness to insult the Buddha in response to a theological invocation at a press conference is perfectly in character. The economy is superb: one sentence destroys the argument by attacking the messenger (and his body, which is ironic given the context). Very quotable.

jokes.text: 'I write a lot of treatii.' / 'Treatises, Ray. The normal plural.'
jokes.setup: Ray tries to contextualize his blog post as one of many academic works.
jokes.punchline: He uses 'treatii' as the plural of treatises — a Latinate plural invented for a word that doesn't need one — and Mike corrects him with barely contained exasperation.
jokes.explanation: A great callback to Ray's earlier 'I love words very ardently' — he clearly doesn't. 'Treatii' is a perfect invented non-word, and Mike's correction 'The normal plural' perfectly captures the exasperation of a professional writer dealing with an amateur. Also foreshadows the Siri callback later.

jokes.text: 'I have always been a friend of the fa... full-figured folks.'
jokes.setup: Selina is asked if she hates fat people at the press conference.
jokes.punchline: Her attempt to say 'fat people' is audibly abandoned mid-word in favor of 'full-figured folks' — and the self-correction is more damning than the original word would have been.
jokes.explanation: The audible self-correction — the 'fa...' hanging in the air — is excellent cringe comedy. It reveals that Selina's PC language is purely performative and applied under pressure. 'Full-figured folks' as a replacement is also funny: alliterative and patronizing. A great performance moment written into the script.

jokes.text: Okay, my pretend friends. I gotta go back to DC. Something very big has happened and I've gotta be there. / What is it? / Daniwah!
jokes.setup: Selina is escaping the press conference. Jonah appears and is asked what happened.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's response to the question about the very big thing is 'Daniwah!' — suggesting either he genuinely doesn't know or is trolling Selina with her own catchphrase.
jokes.explanation: A good use of the running gag in a moment of tension — defuses and extends the joke without wearing it out.

jokes.text: Daniwah! Easy, buddy! Get your hands off me, all right? I don't want to catch your fucking idiocy. Oh, he's having a heart attack. Are you having a heart attack? No, I think he's just having a breakdown.
jokes.setup: Dan is in crisis at the press conference — chest pain, panic.
jokes.punchline: The 'Daniwah' chant in the background as Dan collapses is the running gag's darkest deployment. 'I don't want to catch your fucking idiocy' is his panicked non-sequitur response to being touched. Mike and Amy's clinical 'heart attack vs. breakdown' debate while he suffers is the payoff.
jokes.explanation: The Daniwah callback in Dan's collapse scene is perfectly timed. 'I don't want to catch your fucking idiocy' is a great panicked Dan line. The heart attack/breakdown debate shows how little anyone cares about him.

jokes.text: Dan possibly having a heart attack: 'No, no, no. I don't panic. I'm the fucking iceman. That's why I'm the campaign manager.'
jokes.setup: Dan appears to be having a panic attack/cardiac event and is being assessed.
jokes.punchline: He insists he doesn't panic as evidence that he can't be having a panic attack — while clearly panicking — and invokes his job title as proof of his composure.
jokes.explanation: Peak Dan — his self-image is so strong that even a medical crisis becomes an opportunity to assert his brand. 'The fucking iceman' as self-description during a panic attack is perfect irony. High character integration because this is entirely specific to Dan's pathology.

jokes.text: What's the code you put before the number? / 0-0-1, U.S. / I just have her name, so I need to memorize the number before I put the codes in.
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to call Sue in a medical emergency but doesn't know international dialing codes.
jokes.punchline: He can't call Sue because he has her in his phone by name and can't figure out how to add the international code — an absurdly mundane technical obstacle in an emergency.
jokes.explanation: Gary's technological helplessness in a crisis is good character comedy — he can retrieve any physical object but can't navigate phone settings. The specific obstacle (memorizing number, then adding code) is believably dumb.

jokes.text: People think, 'He's a fitness guy, how can he know about politics?' I get you, Kent. / Again, I don't think you do. [Ray continues demonstrating he doesn't get it]
jokes.setup: Kent is trying to explain to Ray why his beliefs are damaging — that people question his fitness to advise a politician.
jokes.punchline: Ray confidently declares 'I get you, Kent' immediately before saying something that proves he doesn't. Kent's 'Again, I don't think you do' is the deadpan confirmation.
jokes.explanation: The running pattern of Ray claiming to understand and then demonstrating he doesn't is consistent and well-deployed. Kent's patience and Ray's impervious confidence are a good double act.

jokes.text: And I will go on to say that even I, as a young teenager, had a bit of a struggle with my weight... [extended pause as Ray looks around the room] ...as I'm sure almost everyone here in this room has had at one moment or another in their life.
jokes.setup: Ray is trying to demonstrate empathy for overweight people.
jokes.punchline: He looks around the room and appears to be sizing up whether the people present are overweight before including them in his weight-struggle solidarity — the pause is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The pause (implied by scene timestamps and the phrasing) where Ray appears to visually assess the room before deciding to include them is a great inferred visual beat. The comedy is in the gap — the moment where his obliviousness briefly flickers toward awareness before he bulldozes through with the inclusive statement anyway.

jokes.text: Ray's public confession of his teenage weight struggle — building to the revelation that his 'very biggest' was 151-152 pounds.
jokes.setup: Ray is trying to demonstrate empathy for overweight people by sharing his own struggle.
jokes.punchline: His 'enormous' teenage weight, built up with considerable drama, turns out to be approximately 151 pounds — a weight most adults would not consider a struggle.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed escalation and deflation. The buildup — 'even I, as a young teenager, had a bit of a struggle' — promises a confession; '151 or 152 pounds' is the perfect anti-climax. The room's horrified/bemused reaction ('that's a lot') amplifies the comedy.

jokes.text: This is like the Queen's china. They're never gonna let us back into England again. [Someone breaks china] / Shh! It doesn't matter. / It does matter! This is the fuckin' Queen's china! / Shh!
jokes.setup: The team is in what appears to be a room with the Queen's china while managing a crisis.
jokes.punchline: Something gets broken; Mike's horror at breaking royal property escalates while Amy tries to suppress his panic — the political crisis and the china crisis are treated with equal urgency.
jokes.explanation: A good physical comedy moment (inferred from transcript) — the parallel urgency of political disaster and royal china destruction is characteristically Veep. Mike's horror is a nice character beat.

jokes.text: You really don't get it, so I'm going to keep this very simple. You're fired. / I get you. Very simple. / Great. I'm so pleased we had this talk. Let's see where we all are in a month or so.
jokes.setup: Kent fires Ray with crystalline simplicity after an extended failed explanation.
jokes.punchline: Ray responds as if the firing were a productive mentorship session — scheduling a check-in 'in a month or so.'
jokes.explanation: The full payoff of the Kent/Ray dynamic. Kent's attempt to fire Ray as clearly as possible, and Ray's complete reframing of it as a productive conversation, is a beautiful comic construction. 'Let's see where we all are in a month or so' from someone literally just fired is perfectly delusional. Justifies 8+ as the best Kent/Ray beat in the episode.

jokes.text: What a fuckin' misery marathon that was.
jokes.setup: Selina surveys the wreckage of the day's events.
jokes.punchline: 'Misery marathon' is a perfectly compressed description of everything that has gone wrong — a compound noun that captures both the length and the suffering.
jokes.explanation: 'Misery marathon' is punchy Selina-speak — economical, self-pitying, and instantly relatable. A good throwaway line.

jokes.text: Dan had a nervous collapse. Amy's taking him to the hospital. Ben went back to DC. Kent's firing Ray. And I'm in charge. / Fuck! / I know, right?
jokes.setup: Selina asks where everyone is.
jokes.punchline: Mike's list of disasters ends with 'And I'm in charge' — the most alarming development of all, confirmed by Selina's 'Fuck!' and Mike's 'I know, right?'
jokes.explanation: Mike's 'I know, right?' agreement with Selina's horror at his own competence is perfectly self-aware. The list structure — disasters building to 'I'm in charge' as the punchline — is well-constructed. Mike's agreement that his own leadership is alarming is great character comedy.

jokes.text: Ma'am, the House of Commons voted to censure U.S. data monitoring activities. Huh? And there's five fat kids outside dressed as Satan.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to manage the meltdown.
jokes.punchline: Gary delivers two pieces of catastrophic news back to back — a parliamentary censure vote and then 'five fat kids dressed as Satan' — treating both with equal gravity.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the 'fat kids as Satan' plan is perfectly timed — the callback to the reporter's scheme arriving simultaneously with the censure vote. The juxtaposition of a diplomatic crisis and devil-costumed children is classically Veep. High impact. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Fat people don't even vote. They can't even be bothered to get out of the house, you know? There's no food in the voting booth.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to reassure Selina that people love a weight-loss story.
jokes.punchline: Selina refutes the political value of overweight voters by arguing they won't leave the house to vote — then anchors the reasoning in 'there's no food in the voting booth.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Selina lines. The logic is internally consistent and darkly hilarious: fat people won't vote because there's no food in the booth. It's offensive, confident, and entirely Selina — someone who will say anything if she thinks it's a winning argument. The three-beat structure (can't be bothered/get out of house/no food) builds perfectly. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: God, I hate this stupid country so much. Nothing makes sense here. You ask what temperature it is, you get some tiny little number. Even the plugs here are bigger than the things that they power.
jokes.setup: Selina is frustrated and venting about England.
jokes.punchline: Her specific observations about metric temperature and British plug size reveal a mind that deals with geopolitical complexity by fixating on inconveniences.
jokes.explanation: The British plug observation is specific and correct — British plugs ARE unusually large relative to what they power — which makes it funnier than generic anti-British complaints. Selina's specific frustrations reveal character.

jokes.text: I just got 'Brit-fucked' by that balloon animal.
jokes.setup: Selina is describing what Ray has done to her politically.
jokes.punchline: 'Brit-fucked' as a verb and 'balloon animal' as Ray's descriptor are two invented coinages in one sentence — both are apt and sharp.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Brit-fucked' is a genuinely original verb construction that perfectly captures the experience of being politely, methodically, inescapably destroyed in a British diplomatic setting. 'Balloon animal' as Ray's epithet is equally inspired — it captures his empty colorfulness. The economy is exceptional: two words do the work of a paragraph. Near the top of the episode's rankings. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Dan only hired him as a sex slave. / What? / Dan, he hired Ray as a... as a sex slave, did you not know?
jokes.setup: Selina and Amy are discussing Ray being fired/gone.
jokes.punchline: Amy reveals that Dan hired Ray originally as a 'sex slave' for Selina — a piece of information delivered as if it were common knowledge that Selina should already have.
jokes.explanation: A great reveal with an excellent structure — Amy's matter-of-fact delivery of 'sex slave' and then the incredulous re-delivery 'did you not know?' implies the entire staff knew this about Selina's own hired consultant. The power dynamic reversal (Selina is the last to know about her own love life logistics) is very funny.

jokes.text: He... he pimped me out? [long pause] Yeah, yeah.
jokes.setup: Selina processes the news that Dan arranged Ray as a sex arrangement.
jokes.punchline: The pause before Amy's flat 'yeah, yeah' confirmation — and Selina's dawning horror — is the joke. Amy's tone suggests she finds discussing her boss's sex life as tedious as any other administrative matter.
jokes.explanation: The pause and Amy's 'yeah, yeah' are the perfect button on the reveal. Amy's casual confirmation of 'he pimped you out' with zero emotional investment is excellent character comedy — she has clearly processed this information and moved on. A textbook reaction beat.

jokes.text: Who knows about this? / Uh, me, Amy and Mike. / Okay. / And Ben, Kent and Sue. Everybody. / Everybody pretty much knows, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina asks how many people know about the Dan/Ray arrangement.
jokes.punchline: Gary starts small ('me, Amy and Mike') and expands to encompass the entire senior staff — 'everybody pretty much knows, ma'am.'
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure (small list expanding to 'everybody') is well-executed. Gary being the one to deliver this — clearly having known and said nothing — is good character work.

jokes.text: I gotta say, Amy. You're like an actual friend. I mean, throughout all this... you know, me being made campaign manager over you... I mean, you could have been a real bitch, and you've been great.
jokes.setup: Dan is being transported to hospital and attempts to express gratitude to Amy.
jokes.punchline: His 'compliment' to Amy is that she 'could have been a real bitch' — framing generosity as the absence of bitchiness, while also reminding her he was promoted over her.
jokes.explanation: Dan at his most characteristically awful — expressing gratitude in a way that contains both a condescension and a boast. 'You could have been a real bitch' is both a compliment and an insult. The vulnerability of his medical situation makes his inability to just be kind extra funny.

jokes.text: You know, me being made campaign manager over you... I mean, you could have been a real bitch, and you've been great.
jokes.setup: Dan, in the hospital, is thanking Amy for being supportive despite losing the campaign manager role to him.
jokes.punchline: 'You could have been a real bitch, and you've been great' — the backhanded compliment is so Dan: gratitude wrapped in an insult, delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Peak Dan — a genuine moment of vulnerability immediately wrapped in an insult. The 'real bitch' framing is perfectly him; the 'you've been great' is almost touching until you remember how he just framed it.

jokes.text: Am I hallucinating? Can I please get some better drugs?
jokes.setup: Jonah appears at Dan's hospital bedside.
jokes.punchline: Dan's first response to seeing Jonah is to question his own sanity and request better medication — treating Jonah's presence as a medical symptom.
jokes.explanation: A very clean joke — Jonah as hallucination is the perfect medical-context insult. The specificity of asking for 'better drugs' (implying the current ones aren't strong enough to explain Jonah) is nicely economical.

jokes.text: What, are you molesting coma patients? Is that a thing? I might, now. Maybe I'll just put them in some funny hats.
jokes.setup: Dan asks Jonah what he's doing at the hospital.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's escalation from 'I might' to 'maybe I'll put them in funny hats' — treating coma patient molestation as a spontaneous hobby to consider — is pure Jonah.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'molesting coma patients' to 'putting them in funny hats' as a cheerful alternative is a weird and specific escalation. The hat callback (Selina's hat earlier) is possibly unintentional but adds a layer.

jokes.text: Those flowers aren't for Dan? Oh, fuck, no. No, those are for my 'filthy Mary Poppins' and I'm gonna give her a 'spoonful of sugar.'
jokes.setup: Amy notices Jonah has brought flowers to the hospital.
jokes.punchline: They're for his date with the reporter — 'filthy Mary Poppins / spoonful of sugar' builds on the earlier Mary Poppins callbacks to a crescendo of inappropriate imagery.
jokes.explanation: The Mary Poppins running gag reaches full deployment here. 'Filthy Mary Poppins' + 'spoonful of sugar' as a sexual euphemism is highly quotable and earns its callback status. Good structural payoff to the earlier Poppins thread.

jokes.text: Selina just fired you as campaign manager, so take as long as you need. Take longer.
jokes.setup: Amy has visited Dan in hospital and starts to leave.
jokes.punchline: She wishes him well, reveals he's been fired, and then inverts 'get well soon' into 'take longer' — wishing him extended illness since he has no job to return to.
jokes.explanation: An excellent line — the inversion of 'get well soon' to 'take longer' is structurally perfect. Amy holds the news until the last possible moment and then uses it to restructure the standard hospital farewell into a devastating exit. The character satisfaction (Amy finally with an upper hand over Dan) gives it extra impact. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Do you realize how difficult and demeaning it was for me to set up that meeting? / And it was a triumph, Sue. / You have put me in a hideous situation. Remotely, I might add. So from now on, remotely is how we will interact.
jokes.setup: Selina has to cancel Prince Charles after Sue went to enormous trouble to set up the meeting.
jokes.punchline: Sue declares that henceforth they will only interact remotely — as punishment for the indignity of canceling Charles — delivered with total bureaucratic gravity.
jokes.explanation: 'Remotely is how we will interact' as a punishment for professional indignity is peak Sue — her personal dignity is a matter of supreme importance and she enforces it through bureaucratic channels. High character integration: this is entirely specific to Sue's unique mode of passive-aggressive formality.

jokes.text: My flight doesn't leave until tomorrow night, so I figured it makes sense for me to be on Air Force Two.
jokes.setup: Jonah appears at Air Force Two as Selina is trying to leave.
jokes.punchline: His 'logic' — that because his flight is tomorrow, he should therefore be on Air Force Two — is its own kind of deranged argument.
jokes.explanation: Classic Jonah sense of entitlement — his presence on Air Force Two is so obviously wrong that only Jonah would state it as if logical.

jokes.text: My flight doesn't leave until tomorrow night, so I figured it makes sense for me to be on Air Force Two. / Uh, in what world would that make sense? You need to get on your running machine, and run away. / Running machines don't go anywhere.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to hitch a ride on Air Force Two back to DC.
jokes.punchline: Selina tells him to get on his 'running machine' (treadmill) and run away — a British-ism she's picked up. Jonah's literal response — 'running machines don't go anywhere' — is either an intentional troll or genuine stupidity.
jokes.explanation: 'Running machines don't go anywhere' as a literal response to a metaphorical instruction is a perfect Jonah line — it's impossible to tell if he's being wilfully obtuse or genuinely confused. Either reading is funny. Good economy.

jokes.text: The First Lady has attempted to take her own life. / Oh... what?! / How? / Overdose of sleeping pills and vodka. / That's why POTUS sent for Ben.
jokes.setup: Gary says he has bad news. The episode has been a series of disasters.
jokes.punchline: The news is the First Lady's suicide attempt — a genuinely dark escalation that reframes all previous problems as trivial. The matter-of-fact delivery of 'sleeping pills and vodka' by Gary is the darkly comic element.
jokes.explanation: Veep's darkest joke in the episode — the FLOTUS storyline pays off the earlier cattiness about her weight with a suicide attempt, recontextualizing it as something Selina should have noticed. The deadpan information delivery is what makes it comedy rather than pure drama. The callback gives it structural weight.

jokes.text: Really makes you put your own problems into perspective. Doesn't it? Seriously? / It does. / Although your problems are still pretty bad. / Yeah, they are.
jokes.setup: Just after hearing the First Lady has attempted suicide.
jokes.punchline: Selina starts to reflect on perspective — then Gary immediately adds 'although your problems are still pretty bad,' and Selina agrees. The moment of humanity lasts approximately two seconds.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. Gary's 'although your problems are still pretty bad' immediately after a suicide-attempt revelation, and Selina's sincere 'yeah, they are,' is both deeply funny and darkly revealing about both characters. The structure — build-up of perspective, immediate deflation — is near-perfect. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: I couldn't help but overhear, but I always thought that lady was a little damaged. / Jesus fucking Christ. You're fired.
jokes.setup: Selina has just heard about the First Lady's suicide attempt. Ray is in the room and has been eavesdropping.
jokes.punchline: Ray offers his wellness-coach diagnosis of the First Lady ('a little damaged') after her suicide attempt — and gets immediately fired with 'Jesus fucking Christ.'
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of the best in the show. Every element is perfect: Ray's obliviousness reaching its terminal point ('a little damaged' about a woman who just attempted suicide), the timing (immediately after the revelation), Selina's three-word firing ('Jesus fucking Christ. You're fired.'), and the structural payoff of the entire Ray arc. It's dark, earned, specific to Ray's character, and delivered with maximum economy. This is what the whole episode has been building toward. Easily the episode's standout moment and approaches the 9+ anchor.

jokes.text: Let's get the merrie olde fuck out of merrie olde England. I need to be driven to the airport at 'Diana speed,' okay? Just more carefully, though, please.
jokes.setup: Selina wants to leave England immediately.
jokes.punchline: 'Diana speed' — referencing Princess Diana's fatal car crash — immediately qualified by 'just more carefully, though, please,' which is simultaneously the darkest and most self-aware joke Selina makes all episode.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8.5+ easily. 'Diana speed' is an extraordinarily original and dark coinage — invoking a royal tragedy as a unit of velocity measurement in England, of all places. The immediate self-correction ('just more carefully, though, please') is both a punchline and a reveal of Selina's self-preservation instinct activating. The structure is perfect: the joke lands, then it lands again. One of the show's great exit lines.

jokes.text: Amy reading Dan's fake medical chart: 'Dan Egan, 67, female. Unemployed campaign manager. 30 different types of semen pumped from stomach, inverted nipples, abnormally high douche readings... Cancer of the soul, traces of dog excrement found around the corners of the mouth, chronic cretinism, leprosy, anal bleeding... tiny child balls.'
jokes.setup: The episode's coda: Amy reads from what appears to be Dan's medical chart.
jokes.punchline: The chart is clearly fabricated (or a fantasy) — a list of escalating insults disguised as medical diagnoses, building from mundane ('unemployed') through grotesque ('30 types of semen') to absurdly specific ('tiny child balls').
jokes.explanation: A bravura list joke that ends the episode. The structure is excellent — it starts plausible ('67, female' being wrong), escalates through grotesque specificity ('30 types of semen,' 'dog excrement around the corners of the mouth'), and ends on the perfect anti-climax of 'tiny child balls.' 'Cancer of the soul' and 'chronic cretinism' are particular highlights. The escalation and landing is textbook comedy list construction. Justifies 8.5+ as a sustained, well-crafted comedic set piece.

tmdb_overview: In the U.K. for the 100th anniversary of WWI, Selina’s staff worries about Ray’s growing influence on the Veep. Meanwhile, Dan’s nerves begin to fray as Ben gets news that sends him back to D.C. Later, Jonah arrives in London, U.K. to spy for Team Maddox, and Amy leaks a secret.
insight: 102 jokes in 39 minutes—Veep's densest episode maintains character chaos over setup reliance.
